From De Loecker, Eeckhout, Unger (2019 WP)

Other Estimates

Flynn, Gandhi, and Traina (2019 WP)


The U.S. Automobile Industry

US Auto Manufacturing

  • 3% of GDP (~$600 billion)
  • ~ 250,000 employees

 \,

Auto Imports

  • $340 billion.

 \,

Secondary Market

  • ~$350 billion

Research questions

What are trends in market power in the auto industry?

 \,

How is market power related to trends in

  • concentration,
  • product proliferation and differentiation,
  • import competition,
  • product innovations?

Strategy

Production function methods potentially under-equipped at telling us "why?" market power changes.


We estimate supply and demand for new cars from 1982-2017.


Features of the model can help decompose sources of market power.

Explain DLEU markup decomposition.

Data

Cars

All light vehicles sold in US from 1982-2017

  • prices
  • quantities
  • characteristics
  • production country
  • refresh design years

Demographics (CEX)

  • income
  • family size
  • ?

Trends in the Auto Industry, last 40 years

Summary: It's complicated.

Prices (2015 $USD)

HHI

Counts of Brands and Products

Counts of Products by Style

Summary

  1. Concentration going down.
  2. Prices going up.

Quality

  • Features like AC, safety, fuel efficiency.
  • Durability.

Model

Demand

Canonical discrete choice model of product differentiation.

  • Car and household characteristics interactions.

 \,

Supply

Infer marginal costs and markups using static Nash Bertrand first order conditions.

Empirical Specification and Estimation

Three specifications

  1. Simple Logit
  2. Model 1: Price X Income interaction
  3. Model 2: + Constant X Income interaction

Identifying Elasticities: Real Exchange Rates

Price level of expenditure in assembly country.


PPPiXRi=PPP rel. to USnominal XR\frac{PPP_i}{XR_i} = \frac{\text{PPP rel. to US}}{\text{nominal } XR}XRi​PPPi​​=nominal XRPPP rel. to US​
 \,

  1. Wages ↑ ⇢ RealXR ↑ via PPP ⇢ Price ↑
  2. Nominal XR ↑ ⇢ currency depreciation ⇢ Price ↓
